title: Listening - Part 4: Designing Costumes for Film
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Maya, who designs costumes for films and television. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: This afternoon we’re joined by Maya Shah, a costume designer for film and television. Maya, did you always sew your own clothes? || Maya: My mother could sew, but I was impatient and usually bought clothes like everyone else. My interest began through drama at school. [24]When our play had almost no budget, I altered clothes from charity shops so the actors could look as if they belonged in a different period[/24]. I loved creating a character before the actor even spoke. || Presenter: You’ve worked in both theatre and film. Are they very different? || Maya: They share the same imagination, but film changed my attention to detail. In a theatre audience, a button may be invisible. [25]On camera, a small seam or modern zip can be seen in close-up, so every detail has to support the story[/25]. That can be demanding, but it is fascinating too. || Presenter: What was difficult about your first professional job? || Maya: I expected creative pressure. I didn’t expect the speed. Actors might need a costume adjusted before filming continues. [26]I had to learn to make accurate alterations extremely quickly while a whole crew was waiting[/26]. I made one mistake early on, but a senior designer showed me how to stay calm and prepare better. || Presenter: How do you research historical costumes? || Maya: I look at paintings and photographs, but I also visit museums when possible. It isn’t only about copying a dress. [27]Seeing real garments helps me understand how heavy they were and how they allowed, or prevented, the wearer from moving[/27]. That knowledge affects how an actor stands and walks in costume. || Presenter: Is the industry becoming more environmentally responsible? || Maya: Slowly, yes. Film productions once bought large amounts of new fabric for costumes used only briefly. [28]Now I’m often asked to adapt clothes from previous productions or reuse fabric rather than ordering everything new[/28]. It saves money as well, but the environmental reason matters to me most. || Presenter: Do actors have much influence on what they wear? || Maya: They should. An outfit may look perfect on my drawing, but an actor needs to perform in it for hours. [29]Actors often point out that a sleeve or shoe changes the way they can move, and their feedback helps me improve the costume[/29]. Costume design is a collaboration, not a private art project. || Presenter: What should students include in a portfolio? || Maya: Lovely final photographs are useful, but they are not enough. [30]Show how you solved practical problems, such as making an outfit look expensive with limited materials or allowing a performer to move safely[/30]. Employers want to know how you think, not just what you can decorate. || Presenter: Maya, many thanks. || Maya: Thank you.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first interested Maya in costume design?

options.A: Making outfits for a school production with little money.
options.B: Learning traditional sewing from her mother.
options.C: Designing modern clothes for friends to buy.

questions.explanation: Maya became interested when she changed second-hand clothes for a low-budget school play.

questions.question: What does Maya say about designing for film?

options.A: Costumes need to be comfortable above all else.
options.B: Small details are much more noticeable.
options.C: Actors require a greater variety of outfits.

questions.explanation: She explains that close-up film shots reveal tiny details such as seams and zips.

questions.question: What did Maya need to learn in her first professional job?

options.A: How to work quickly without losing accuracy.
options.B: How to communicate with difficult actors.
options.C: How to make original costumes from drawings.

questions.explanation: She had to carry out accurate alterations rapidly while a crew waited.

questions.question: Why does Maya study real historical garments?

options.A: To find materials that are still available today.
options.B: To avoid creating costumes similar to museum pieces.
options.C: To understand how clothing influenced movement.

questions.explanation: Real garments help her understand weight and freedom of movement.

questions.question: How is Maya's work becoming more sustainable?

options.A: Productions increasingly use fabric and costumes again.
options.B: Films now require fewer costumes for each character.
options.C: Designers mainly purchase fabric from local suppliers.

questions.explanation: She is often asked to adapt old costumes or reuse existing fabric.

questions.question: Why does Maya value actors' opinions?

options.A: They know which costumes look best on screen.
options.B: They can explain how clothing affects performance.

questions.explanation: Actors tell her how an item affects their movement, helping her improve it.

questions.question: What should students demonstrate in a portfolio?

options.A: A broad knowledge of fashion history.
options.B: An ability to create attractive drawings.
options.C: An ability to deal with practical challenges.

questions.explanation: Maya advises showing solutions to restrictions involving materials, cost and safety.
